questionText: The lengths, $X$ mm, of leaves on a plant are modelled by $X \sim N(50, 4^2)$. Find $P(X < 58)$, giving your answer to $4$ significant figures.

steps.description: State the distribution and what is required
steps.explanation: The lengths follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 50$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 4$, because the second number is the variance $4^2 = 16$. We want the chance that a leaf is shorter than $58$ mm.

To use the standard normal we turn $X$ into a $z$-score by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation. A $z$-score simply counts how many standard deviations a value sits away from the mean.
The length $58$ is $8$ mm above the mean, and $8$ is exactly two standard deviations of $4$. So $58$ mm corresponds to $z = 2$.

steps.description: Rewrite the probability in terms of Z
steps.explanation: Standardising keeps the order of values, so ‘$X$ below $58$’ is the same event as ‘$Z$ below $2$’. This lower-tail area is exactly the standard normal cumulative value $\Phi(2)$.
Reading the standard normal cumulative distribution on a calculator (or from tables) at $z = 2$ gives $0.9772$ to $4$ significant figures.
So about $97.7\%$ of leaves are shorter than $58$ mm. This is close to $1$, which is sensible because $58$ mm lies well to the right of the mean, so almost all leaves fall below it.
